questions.question: How should each rescue breath be delivered?
options: A. As forcefully and quickly as possible
B. Over about 1 second, just enough to produce visible chest rise
C. Over 5 seconds with maximal volume
questions.rationale: Each breath is given over about 1 second with only enough volume to make the chest visibly rise. Larger or faster breaths cause harm through over-ventilation and gastric inflation.

questions.question: Which airway maneuver is preferred when a cervical spine injury is suspected?
questions.rationale: The jaw-thrust opens the airway while minimizing neck movement, making it preferred when a spinal injury is suspected.

questions.question: The E-C clamp technique is used to:
options: B. Maintain a mask seal while lifting the jaw during bag-mask ventilation
questions.rationale: The E-C clamp uses the thumb and index finger (C) to seal the mask and the remaining fingers (E) to lift the jaw, maintaining a good seal for bag-mask ventilation.

questions.question: Why is bag-mask ventilation usually more effective with two rescuers?
options: A. It doubles the oxygen supply
B. One rescuer maintains a two-handed mask seal while the other squeezes the bag
D. It eliminates the need to open the airway
questions.rationale: A two-handed seal by one rescuer greatly reduces leaks, while the second rescuer provides consistent bag volume — together delivering far more effective breaths than a one-person technique.

questions.question: What is the adult rescue-breathing rate for a patient with a pulse who is not breathing adequately?
questions.rationale: For an adult with a pulse but inadequate breathing, deliver 1 breath every 6 seconds, about 10 breaths per minute.

questions.question: What is a consequence of excessive ventilation?
options: B. Gastric inflation and reduced venous return, lowering cardiac output
questions.rationale: Over-ventilation raises intrathoracic pressure (reducing blood return to the heart) and inflates the stomach, increasing vomiting and aspiration risk.

questions.question: When is an oropharyngeal airway (OPA) appropriate?
options: B. Only in a patient without a gag reflex
questions.rationale: An OPA is used only in patients without a gag reflex, because it can provoke gagging, vomiting, and aspiration in a patient who still has one.

questions.question: What advantage does a pocket mask have over a simple face shield?
options: A. It requires no airway opening
B. It has a one-way valve, allows a two-handed seal, and can accept supplemental oxygen
D. It works without a seal
questions.rationale: A pocket mask provides a one-way valve for protection, a better two-handed seal, and often an oxygen inlet, making ventilation more effective and safer than a thin face shield.

questions.question: During ventilation you see no chest rise. What is the best first response?
options: A. Squeeze the bag much harder
B. Reposition the airway and improve the mask seal, then check for obstruction
questions.rationale: No chest rise usually means a poor seal or an unopened/obstructed airway. Reposition the head/jaw, improve the seal, and check for a foreign body — do not simply force more air.

questions.question: Approximately what tidal volume is appropriate for adult bag-mask breaths?
options: B. About 500–600 mL (enough for visible chest rise)
D. As much as the bag holds
questions.rationale: About 500–600 mL produces visible chest rise in an adult without over-inflating; giving the full bag volume risks over-ventilation and gastric inflation.

questions.question: Is routine cricoid pressure recommended during CPR?
options: B. No, it is not routinely recommended
questions.rationale: Routine cricoid pressure is not recommended during CPR, as it can impede ventilation and does not reliably prevent aspiration.

questions.question: With an advanced airway in place, how is ventilation delivered?
options: A. Pause compressions for each breath
B. Continuous compressions with 1 breath every 6 seconds
questions.rationale: With an advanced airway, compressions are continuous and breaths are given once every 6 seconds (about 10/min) without pausing compressions.

questions.question: What should be done before every ventilation attempt?
questions.rationale: The airway must be opened (head-tilt/chin-lift or jaw-thrust) before ventilating; otherwise the tongue can block airflow and breaths will not reach the lungs.

questions.question: A trauma patient needs ventilation and may have a neck injury. Which technique and device pairing is best?
options: B. Jaw-thrust with a bag-mask, ideally two rescuers
C. Hyperextend the neck and use mouth-to-mouth
D. No airway opening, just squeeze the bag
questions.rationale: With a possible neck injury, open the airway using a jaw-thrust (minimizing neck movement) and ventilate with a bag-mask, preferably with two rescuers for a good seal.

questions.question: Which finding indicates ventilation is effective?
options: B. The chest visibly rises with each breath
C. Air escapes around the mask
questions.rationale: Visible chest rise indicates air is reaching the lungs. Gastric/abdominal distension or air leaking around the mask indicates ineffective or misdirected ventilation.

questions.question: Under stress, a rescuer tends to squeeze the bag hard and fast. Why is this harmful?
options: B. It causes over-ventilation and gastric inflation, reducing cardiac output and risking aspiration
questions.rationale: Fast, forceful bagging over-ventilates the patient — raising intrathoracic pressure and inflating the stomach — which reduces circulation and increases the risk of vomiting and aspiration.

questions.question: Which adjunct is better tolerated in a patient who may still have a gag reflex?
questions.rationale: An NPA is generally better tolerated than an OPA when a gag reflex may be present, as the OPA can provoke gagging and vomiting.

questions.question: During single-rescuer CPR with a pocket mask, what ventilation pattern is used?
questions.rationale: Without an advanced airway, CPR uses 30:2 — two breaths, each over about 1 second, after every 30 compressions.
